summary:
The Lombard Kingdom of Italy (568–774 AD) was the last and most lasting of the [[event:2038|Germanic Migration Period]] successor states in Italy — established when the Lombards (a Germanic people from the Danube region) invaded Italy in 568 AD, just fourteen years after [[event:2119|Justinian's]] Gothic Wars had 'reconquered' Italy for Byzantium, and established a kingdom that controlled most of the Italian peninsula (except Byzantine enclaves at Ravenna, Rome, and the south) for over two centuries. The Lombard invasion found Italy devastated by the Gothic Wars and plague, and the population offered little resistance.

The Lombard kingdom was initially hostile to both the papacy (which it threatened) and Byzantium (which it gradually absorbed). Lombard law (the Edictum Rothari, 643 AD) was an early example of Germanic legal codification, and Lombard culture — their gold jewelry, their distinctive helmet style, and their funerary art — was sophisticated. The Lombards gradually converted from Arianism to Nicene Christianity and assimilated with the Latin Roman population; by the 7th century they were thoroughly Romanized in language and religion. The kingdom was destroyed when the Lombards threatened Rome too aggressively, prompting Pope Stephen II to appeal to the Frankish king Pepin III, then [[event:2138|Charlemagne]], who conquered the Lombard kingdom in 774 AD. 'Lombardy' (the Po valley in northern Italy) takes its name from the Lombards, whose tenure was thus long enough to permanently mark the geography.
